title: The Macro Minute: Is the US economy still resilient?

Happy Tuesday out there team 42. It's
your skip here at Darius Dell to present
our macro minute for Tuesday September
16th 2025. As always, we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note. If you would like
to review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk
or to get on the right side of market
risk to the extent you have been
following Kiss or Dr. Mo need to be a
client of 42 macro. So today's key macro
question is is the US economy still
resilient? Our answer yes. The August
retail sales data and industrial
production data generally supported our
resilient US economy theme. While we
still anticipate the worst of the
U-shaped economy lies ahead of us, the
likelihood of the US economy entering an
actual recession over a medium-term time
horizon remains low. And we still
anticipate a better than expected
recovery throughout 2026 that is likely
to continue generally surprising
consensus GDP and earnings estimates to
the upside. This datadriven view is
supported by the latest retail sales and
industrial production data. Recall that
both retail sales and industrial
production are persistent leading
indicators of the business cycle
according to the 42 macro business cycle
model. Elsewhere, an appeals court
blocked the White House from removing
Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook,
allowing her to remain in her position
while her lawsuit challenging Donald
Trump's attempt to fire her moves
forward. The court issued a 2 to1 ruling
just hours before the FOMC's two-day
interest rate meeting. and President
Donald Trump can still petition the
Supreme Court to intervene. DC Circuit
judges J. Michelle Childs and Bradley
Garcia, both appointed by former
President Joe Biden, rejected the
administration's request to let Trump
remove Cook, while Judge Greg Katzus,
appointed by President Trump, descented.
Repeat after me. No institution is safe
from the influence of politics during a
fourth turning.
Meanwhile, the Senate confirmed former
chair of White House economic coun um
White House Council of Economic Advisor
Steve Moran to the Federal Reserve Board
in a 48 to 47 vote on Monday evening.
Senate Republicans fasttracked his
confirmation process, which is yet
another sign of the historic
consolidation of power by President
Trump as he escalates his pressure
campaign for the Fed to cut interest
rates. pus could nominate Moran for a
full 14-year term beginning in February,
select a different nominee, or allow
Moran to remain indefinitely if no
replacement is chosen. It remains our
view that the federal Fed governor seat
Moran is currently occupying will
ultimately become the next Fed chair
because the Trump administration cannot
afford to take any chances regarding
JPAL's upcoming decision to remain on
the board after his chairmanship ends
next May, which would be a historic act
of arguably well-deserved pettiness.
The last Fed chair to continue on the
board of governors after his chair term
ended was Mariner Eckley who served as
chairman from 1934 to 1948 and then
remained a governor until 1951 which
coincidentally is when the yield curve
control the last time the Fed did um
durable yield curve control. That's when
that ended. Recall that they kept um the
short rate at 38 for nine years and the
10ear at uh 2 and a half% for nine
years. for nine years the last time the
US entered uh fiscal dominance. So, uh,
I'm not saying that's going to be the
outcome this time around, but you have
to expect durable monetary debasement
and financial oppression at an
accelerating and exponential up
quantities, sorry, in accelerating
exponential quantities as a result of
this fiscal dominance regime, which
continues to gobble up an unhealthy
amount of the world's savings uh, and is
contributing to all sorts of
distributional problems in the US
economy, problems that will be addressed
at the ballot box uh, in the coming uh,
years. And so you have to position for
that as an investor if your goal is to
retire on time and comfortably or help
your clients retire on time and
comfortably. So transitioning to our 42
macro dashboard as always we'll wrap up
with a question from our community. This
one's titled returns and inflation.
Question says, "Hi Darius and team
quickly. I can't appreciate enough how
helpful your morning notes have been. An
absolute pleasure to read and absorb."
Thank you. Appreciate the kind kind
compliment. Uh I was having a discussion
with my uncle recently who has a wealth
of knowledge in the investing space yet
I believe is still fairly unfamiliar to
the benefits of gold and bitcoin in
diversified portfolio. I raised two
points of contention with him yet due to
my inexperience being only 20 I was
wasn't quite sure what to think. Uh he
was strong in his view that returns of
20 to 30% peranom in a low risk
portfolio was unachievable. After
explaining how family offices at
top-notch firms were able to achieve 8
to 10% low risk. Then I raised the
concern that real inflation was most
likely around that number with real
returns likely being only two to three%
peranom. He then stated that CPI is
roughly 2%. However, I'm unconvinced
that CPI is an accurate measurement of
inflation. Would you say that returns of
that caliber with a lowrisisk portfolio
is possible? And uh how would you
address the concerns uh regarding
inflation? So phenomenal question. This
is the kind of question we get all the
time. Uh, you know, if you think about
who has the money, certainly when you
see it in the statistics, but just
generally speaking, you can see it in
our client base and the client base and
most people who are subscribed to
investment services, they tend to have
gray hair because the great people with
the gray hair have all the money in the
economy. You know, the people with the
the darker hair and the blonder hair
tend not to have money to invest. So, by
and large, investors tend to skew up the
um the age distribution. And so clearly
a lot of investors uh you know
particularly those that have been sold a
uh dying bill of goods in the context of
things like 6040 portfolios or the the
innovation of the 7030 portfolio uh in
terms of uh you know still having way
too much exposure to treasury bonds in a
in a in a regime that does not support
that type of um portfolio construction.
uh you know it's our view and it's been
our view for years now that the
conditions that created the
outperformance of the 6040 portfolio
which allowed investors to have you know
high reasonably high positive returns
with very limited risk no longer exist.
Those conditions no longer exist. They
stopped existing 5 years ago. We were
very we became very clear about that uh
starting in 20 late 2021 and then became
so clear about that in 2023 that we
explicitly told investors to just get
rid of it altogether. And so um in our
view you know that that so this concept
of you know achieving you know high
returns with low risk is in fact
something that is very difficult to do.
Um you know I I tweet I didn't tweet I
sent it out on LinkedIn this morning a
note essentially reminding retail
investors that hey your competitive
advantage is
the ability to collect the money from
the heavens when it's there and to close
your basket when it's not. Institutional
investors have to remain at or near
fully invested. And so they are not able
to sort of you know avoid volatility
drag with you know the kinds of tools
and and the simplicity that we can avoid
volatility drag with you know we can
just choose not to participate uh in
markets uh when we have um you know
volatility um you know sort of big left
tail events developing in financial
markets. uh institutional investors uh
they they can't and so they have to
employ you know things like volatility
targeting things like dynamic position
sizing uh uh to to sort of uh mitigate
volatility drag in their portfolio and
coincidentally those are exactly the
same kind of strategies quantitative
strategies that we have implemented into
our KISS and Dr. O risk management
overlays to uh to to keep them on the
right side of market risk. But what's
different about KISS specifically
relative to uh you know many of the the
best performing you know kind of hedge
funds and the risk adjusted return basis
across global Wall Street is that you
know we can expose our portfolios to
stocks gold bitcoin in ways that you
know essentially allow us to do three
things. One stock equity exposure allows
you to offensively take advantage of
productivity growth which is something
you want to have your portfolio exposed
to. That is what drives financial
markets over the long term. Um you want
to have your offensive exposure to
productivity growth while having in this
particular regime defensive exposure to
financial oppression, monetary
debasement. Those are the risks. You
know the the the the reason the 6040
portfolio worked for such a long time is
because stocks and bonds were inversely
correlated because the key risk in that
moment in that you know kind of 40 years
of time was deflation. In our opinion
that now the key risk is financial
repression and monetary debasement. It
may not result in, you know, 10 plus
percent infl CPI inflation, but it will
result in asset prices going up much
faster than people who aren't
participating or who aren't um exposed
to those types of uh uh uh instruments
are are likely to participate in. And in
my opinion, I think it's, you know,
asset price inflation is just as
ownorous to you as a as someone who's
trying to retire as CPI inflation. The
the inflation is inflation. Whether it
be asset price inflation or CPI
inflation, we all are aware of CPI
inflation, it's easier to spot and see,
record all this other stuff. But when it
comes to asset price inflation, it's
negative impact on investors if you're
not participating in it is significant.
It's very significant. I mean, think
about it. If your goal is to retire
comfortably, that implies you have to
have more money than the other people to
buy goods and services. If your
portfolio goes up at a much slower rate
than all the other retirees, then you're
going to be able to consume fewer goods
and services than they will in the
future. You will be able to buy less
houses. You'll be able to buy less, you
know, trips. You'll be able to buy less
things. And ultimately, you'll be able
to transfer less money to the people you
leave behind when you go to the great
beyond. And so you know when you part of
investing is is quantitative in terms of
having sophisticated risk management
overlays to keep you on the right side
of market risk but also part of
investing is fundamental to understand
hey what is actually the key risk we
want to be exposed to over the long term
and what do we need to be defending
against over the long term and again
it's productivity growth is the exposed
the offensive part of your portfolio.
It's financial pressure and monetary
debasement that you need to outrun if
you want to retire on time and
comfortably as an investor uh in in this
particular uh regime. That may not
always be the case. We may eventually at
some point, I'm not that old, you know,
maybe at some point in my career, we'll
go back to deflation being the dominant
risk in financial markets and the stock
bond correlation going back to inverse
again and ultimately, you know,
something like the 6040 portfolio will
return and rise from the ashes. But in
our opinion, we don't think that outcome
is anytime soon. And so in this
particular moment of time on the x- axis
for the next, you know, however many
years it lasts, you need to be aware
that the key risks you need to protect
yourself against in the in investing is
not deflation. It is inflation, CPI
inflation, asset price inflation, which
are ultimately the flip side of the same
coin of monetary debasement and
financial repression. And so hopefully
uh everything you've seen in the hit the
tape, including what we just talked
about in the executive summary, uh
everything you're seeing in terms of the
erosion of central bank independence is
helping you build more confidence in
those types of um in this type of line
of thinking. uh you know and to the
extent you don't have enough confidence
in this uh line of thinking let me
remind you guys for those who may be new
that this KISS portfolio which features
allegedly more risky asset classes like
gold and bitcoin versus treasury bonds
allegedly the kiss portfolio is still
significantly more risk-free or not
risk- free significantly less risky
significantly less risky than the 6040
portfolio again the the kiss portfolio
has a Calmar ratio of 2.2 which is the
average annual return divided by the max
target on absolute absolute return
basis. The calmar ratio for the 6040
portfolio is only 0.5. If you look at
the downside capture ratio of KISS
relative to 6040 it's only about 50%.
You're getting two and a half times the
return but only 50% of the downside
which is why the calar ratio for KISS is
more than four times the calar ratio of
the 60/40 portfolio. Again this is you
know we can't stress this enough. You
have choices to make as a retail
investor. Some of these choices create
competitive advantages for you relative
to institutional investors. You don't
have to take factor risk. You can get in
and out of the markets. And so
ultimately, those are two things you
need to be weaponizing as a retail
investor against Wall Street, which has
billions of dollars and, you know,
millions of Ivy League degrees, you
know, taking the other side of your
trades. You don't want that. So use your
competitive advantage to your to your
benefit and to your family's benefit by
ultimately, you know, partnering with
folks like us who can help, you know,
deliver these kinds of outcomes for you.
You know, you don't have to take our
word for it. Go to our testimonials page
and review hundreds of testimonials from
our thousands of clients around the
world that are all telling you the exact
same thing that I get on this program
and tell you every other day, which is
that case is an incredibly effective
solution. I've not come across a better
solution to help investors retire on
time and comfortably. So, I built it.
I was like, "Well, I I have money now. I
need to retire on time and comfortably."
And I looked around and I couldn't find
anything. So, I built it and that's why
I use KISS to manage my entire liquid
net worth. So, you don't have to use
Kiss to manage your entire liquid net
worth. You can take a sliver of your
portfolio and allocate it to KISS and
keep doing what you're currently doing
with the rest of your portfolio and have
that rotate to more towards KISS over
time. if KISS is outperforming what
you're currently doing or you know
cancel any free you know without penalty
anytime without penalty if KISS isn't
working for you but you know just know
that you know we're creating the

the uh person's uncle uh is uh is
holding unnecessarily in this particular
uh regime of monetary debasement
